columns.semantic_meaning: Self-reported / administratively recorded patient race category. A pre-discharge demographic attribute used as a weak clinical covariate and, more importantly, as a protected attribute for the Fairlearn fairness audit (slicing variable).
columns.value_interpretation: 5 observed levels, heavily skewed: Caucasian 76,099 (74.778%) and AfricanAmerican 19,210 (18.877%) hold ~93.7% of non-missing mass; the tail is sparse: Hispanic 2,037 (2.002%), Other 1,506 (1.48%), Asian 641 (0.63%). Asian/Other/Hispanic together are ~4.1% of rows, so their one-hot cells are statistically thin. No constant, no placeholder beyond '?' (already NaN-loaded). All cited counts/pcts verified against demographic.json.
columns.missing_mechanism_rationale: 2,273 rows (2.234%) are '?'. The rationale the analyst gives ('absence tracks intake workflow, not the value or clinical state') actually describes MCAR-like behaviour, and they concede MCAR cannot be ruled out. I retain MAR as the conservative call (race plausibly correlates with admission_type/source, which are observed), but flag that the mechanism is genuinely MCAR-vs-MAR ambiguous, not 'high confidence' as originally stated. Co-missingness for race is near-zero against every other column (weight -0.025, payer_code -0.048, medical_specialty 0.009, diag_3 0.024, max_glu_serum 0.019, A1Cresult -0.007 — all verified), so there is no shared MNAR mechanism with payer/specialty. Either way the handling (explicit Unknown level) is identical, so the mechanism label is low-stakes here.
columns.target_relationship: Very weak separator. Per-level positive_rate is tightly clustered around the 11.16% base rate: Caucasian 0.11291, AfricanAmerican 0.11218, Hispanic 0.10407, Other 0.09628, Asian 0.10140 (all verified). target_lift 1.012; the max/min spread is 0.11291/0.09628 = 1.173x and is driven entirely by the tiny Asian/Other cells (n=641, n=1,506), so it is small-n noise, not a real gradient. Marginal predictive value as a feature; its true importance is as a fairness slicing variable.
columns.recommended_strategy: Load '?' as NaN (R1), then map the 2,273 missing rows to an explicit 'Unknown' category — do NOT drop rows and do NOT mode-impute to Caucasian (that would inflate the majority and corrupt the fairness slice sizes). Keep all 5 observed levels plus Unknown. Collapse Asian+Other (optionally +Hispanic) into a single 'Other/Asian' level ONLY for the LR baseline if one-hot sparsity destabilises coefficients; PREFER full granularity for the Fairlearn MetricFrame, and audit the slices on the granular version regardless of model encoding.
columns.imputation_rationale: An explicit 'Unknown' level satisfies R1 (never silently drop the missing flag, never let '?' become a real category by accident) and keeps fairness slice proportions honest. Because missingness is ~2% and non-predictive, the 'Unknown' level itself is a sufficient missing indicator; no separate boolean flag is needed.
columns.modeling_transform: One-hot encode (6 columns incl. Unknown); drop-first for the LR baseline to avoid collinearity; order-irrelevant for trees. Do NOT target-encode race — that leaks the outcome into a protected attribute and complicates the fairness audit.
columns.leakage_or_caveat: Protected attribute: must be carried into the Fairlearn MetricFrame for slicing; consider auditing model performance with and without race as a direct input. No post-discharge leakage. Mode-imputation would silently distort fairness slice sizes. The 1.17x level spread is small-n artefact and must not be read as real predictive signal.
alternatives_considered: Mode-impute to Caucasian (rejected: biases majority and fairness audit)
Drop the 2,273 missing rows (rejected: discards info, shrinks fairness slices, and interacts badly with the grouped patient split)
Target-encode (rejected: leakage onto a protected attribute)
Label the mechanism MCAR (defensible given near-zero co-missingness; MAR retained as the conservative default)

columns.semantic_meaning: Patient sex/gender recorded administratively at registration. Pre-discharge demographic and a protected attribute for the fairness audit.
columns.value_interpretation: Effectively binary: Female 54,708 (53.759%) and Male 47,055 (46.238%), plus a degenerate 'Unknown/Invalid' level of 3 rows (0.003%) with 0 positives. No '?' missing (pct_missing 0.0). 'Unknown/Invalid' is an explicit recorded data-quality artefact, not a NaN. All counts verified against demographic.json.
columns.missing_mechanism_rationale: 0 NaN; the column is fully populated. 'Unknown/Invalid' is an explicit recorded value (not '?'), so it does not enter the co-missingness analysis and there is no missing-data mechanism to classify.
columns.target_relationship: Negligible. Female positive_rate 0.11245 vs Male 0.11062 (verified); both essentially at the 11.16% base rate; target_lift 1.008. The 3-row Unknown/Invalid cell is 0/3 positives and is statistically meaningless. Not a useful standalone predictor; value is for fairness slicing only.
columns.recommended_strategy: Treat as binary Female/Male for the model matrix. CORRECTION to the prior analysis, which was internally contradictory (it recommended folding the 3 rows into an 'Unknown' category while in the SAME entry warning never to create a 3-row 'Unknown' dummy). Resolve this: keep all 3 rows (do NOT drop them — row deletion is unjustified for a demographic cleanup and perturbs the grouped patient split / base-rate accounting), and for the MODEL collapse 'Unknown/Invalid' into the majority via a single is_female / is_male binary so no near-zero-variance dummy exists. For the FAIRNESS frame, keep the original 3-level labelling so the artefact is visible and documented, but do not slice the 3-row group as a reportable cohort.
columns.imputation_rationale: No imputation needed (no NaN). The only real decision is the 3 invalid rows; encoding gender as a single binary indicator silently and correctly absorbs them into the majority for modelling without manufacturing a zero-variance dummy, and without deleting rows. R1's '?'-as-category rule does not apply (there is no '?').
columns.modeling_transform: Single binary indicator (is_female) for both LR and trees. Keep the raw 3-level gender column available to the fairness MetricFrame; do not feed a standalone 'Unknown/Invalid' dummy to the model.
columns.leakage_or_caveat: Protected attribute for fairness; no leakage. Caveat: never create a standalone 'Unknown/Invalid' dummy (3 rows, 0 positives = pure noise, unstable coefficient). Prior analysis's 'optionally drop the 3 rows' is downgraded from 'acceptable' to 'avoid' — prefer collapse-to-binary over row deletion.
alternatives_considered: Keep 'Unknown/Invalid' as its own one-hot level (rejected: 3 rows, zero-variance noise)
Drop the 3 invalid rows (downgraded from 'acceptable' to 'avoid': unjustified row deletion; collapse-to-binary is strictly cleaner)
Fold the 3 into a standalone 'Unknown' level (rejected: contradicts the no-3-row-dummy rule)

columns.semantic_meaning: Patient age as a 10-year decade bucket (e.g. '[70-80)'). Pre-discharge demographic; clinically meaningful (readmission risk and comorbidity load broadly rise with age) and a protected attribute for fairness slicing.
columns.value_interpretation: 10 ordered decade buckets, fully populated (0% missing). Left-skewed toward older patients: [70-80) 26,068 (25.616%), [60-70) 22,483 (22.093%), [50-60) 17,256 (16.957%), [80-90) 17,197 (16.899%) hold the bulk. Pediatric/young buckets are tiny and unstable: [10-20) 691 (0.679%), [0-10) 161 (0.158%). Stored as a string interval — ordinal-but-categorical, not numeric yet. All counts verified.
columns.missing_mechanism_rationale: 0 NaN; every encounter has an age bucket. No missing-data mechanism to classify.
columns.target_relationship: Strongest demographic separator of the four (target_lift 1.276). CORRECTION: the prior analysis called the positive_rate trend 'monotone-ish / largely monotone' — that is overstated and not supported by the numbers. Actual rates are NON-monotone: [0-10) 0.01863, [10-20) 0.05789, [20-30) 0.14243 (a sharp spike, 236/1,657), [30-40) 0.11232, [40-50) 0.10604, [50-60) 0.09666 (a mid-life DIP), [60-70) 0.11128, [70-80) 0.11773, [80-90) 0.12084 (peak among the elderly), [90-100) 0.11099 (drops back). So the real shape is: very low in the youngest two buckets, a [20-30) outlier high, a [50-60) dip, then a gentle rise to [80-90). The clinically clean part is only the strong low rate in [0-10)/[10-20); the middle is roughly flat with a young-adult bump. The signal is real but is NOT a clean monotone age gradient.
columns.recommended_strategy: Map each '[a-b)' string to an ordinal integer (rank 0-9 or decade midpoint 5,15,...,95) so trees can exploit the ordering. CORRECTION to the modeling implication: because the rate is non-monotone (young-adult spike + mid-life dip), do NOT rely on a single linear age term for the LR baseline — use either one-hot dummies or a binned/spline encoding so the [20-30) bump and [50-60) dip are representable; a bare linear coefficient will misfit the curve. For trees, the ordinal integer is sufficient (trees handle non-monotone splits natively). Expose a collapsed young/middle/elderly banding to the fairness MetricFrame.
columns.imputation_rationale: No imputation required (no missingness). Ordinal/integer encoding is justified for trees by the inherent ordering; but the earlier justification ('positive_rate trend is largely monotone, so linear-numeric is efficient') is wrong — the trend is non-monotone, so for the linear baseline prefer one-hot or spline over a single linear term.
columns.modeling_transform: Trees: ordinal integer (rank 0-9 or midpoint). LR baseline: one-hot the 10 buckets OR midpoint with a quadratic/spline term to capture the non-monotone curvature — do NOT use a single linear midpoint term as the only encoding. Optionally collapse to 3 bands for fairness slicing.
columns.leakage_or_caveat: Protected attribute (age) for the fairness audit — must be available for slicing. No post-discharge leakage (age known at admission). Caveat: tiny pediatric buckets ([0-10) n=161, [10-20) n=691) and the [20-30) spike (n=1,657) have unstable rates; do not over-fit them. Do not describe the age-readmission relationship as monotone in the model card.
alternatives_considered: Single linear midpoint term as the only encoding (rejected: non-monotone rate, will misfit)
One-hot all 10 buckets (preferred for the LR baseline given non-monotonicity; slightly less efficient but correct)
Ordinal integer for trees (accepted: trees split non-monotone natively)
Collapse to 3 age bands (acceptable for fairness slicing; loses modeling resolution if used as the sole encoding)

columns.semantic_meaning: Patient weight as a 25-lb interval bucket (e.g. '[75-100)'). A potential clinical covariate (obesity/BMI proxy) but in this dataset almost entirely unrecorded.
columns.value_interpretation: Catastrophically missing: 98,569 of 101,766 rows are '?' (96.858%). Only 3,197 rows (3.142%) carry a value, spread over 9 buckets dominated by [75-100) 1,336 (1.313%) and [50-75) 897 (0.881%); the high buckets are negligible ([175-200) 11 rows, >200 3 rows). The recorded rows are themselves a non-random slice, so even the observed value distribution is not a population estimate. All counts verified.
columns.missing_mechanism_rationale: Weight is charted for only ~3% of encounters. In the co-missingness matrix weight is near-orthogonal to every other heavily-missing column (payer_code 0.044, medical_specialty 0.002, max_glu_serum -0.042, A1Cresult -0.028, race -0.025, diag_3 0.010 — all verified), so its missingness is NOT explained by other observed missing columns, ruling out MAR-via-other-missingness. Whether weight is charted depends on encounter type / unit workflow and plausibly on whether it was clinically relevant — i.e. on the value/context itself — which is MNAR. Regardless of the exact label, at 96.858% missing the variable is unusable as a continuous covariate.
columns.target_relationship: As a value: near-useless. Only 3,197 informative rows; per-bucket positive_rates are noisy small-n: [0-25) 0.16667 (8/48), [25-50) 0.08247 (8/97), main buckets ~0.107-0.117 ([75-100) 0.11452, [50-75) 0.11706, [100-125) 0.10720). The headline target_lift 1.493 is inflated by the tiny [0-25) cell and is NOT trustworthy. CORRECTION/refinement to 'missing_is_signal': the recorded rows readmit at roughly the base rate (~0.107-0.117 vs 11.16%), so the weight_recorded flag's PREDICTIVE value is unproven and likely weak — the flag is worth keeping for R1 compliance and as a possible weak workflow proxy, not because the facts show it separates the target.
columns.recommended_strategy: Do NOT use the weight value as a feature and do NOT impute it (any median/mode fill fabricates 96.9% of the column). Engineer a single binary 'weight_recorded' = 1 where a bucket exists, 0 where '?'. Drop the original 9-level weight column from the modeling matrix. This satisfies R1: the FACT of missingness is retained (as the indicator), nothing is silently dropped, and '?' never becomes a value category.
columns.imputation_rationale: With 96.858% missing there is no defensible value imputation — any fill dominates the column with one fabricated value and adds no information. The retainable content is the missingness flag itself, so encode that (weight_recorded) and discard the value buckets.
columns.modeling_transform: Replace the column with one binary missing-indicator (weight_recorded); drop the raw bucketed weight; do NOT one-hot the 9 sparse value buckets.
columns.leakage_or_caveat: No post-discharge leakage (weight, if charted, is an admission-time vital). Caveats: (1) do NOT report the 1.493 target_lift as meaningful — it is a small-n artefact from the [0-25) cell; (2) do NOT claim weight_recorded carries real predictive signal — the recorded rows sit at base rate; keep the flag mainly for R1 compliance, and let feature importance decide whether it survives.
alternatives_considered: Median/bucket-impute weight (rejected: fabricates 96.9% of the column)
One-hot 9 buckets + Unknown (rejected: ~97% mass in one Unknown level, near-zero-variance value buckets)
Keep raw weight value (rejected: unusable)
Drop the column entirely with no indicator (rejected: violates R1 — silently discards the missingness fact)

columns.semantic_meaning: Integer-coded administrative field for the type/acuity of admission at intake (Emergency, Elective, Urgent, Trauma Center, Newborn), plus three bookkeeping codes that mean the value was unknown: NULL(5), Not Available(6), Not Mapped(8). Fixed at the start of the encounter, so it is intake/discharge-time-available, not post-discharge (R4 clean).
columns.value_interpretation: 8 distinct integer codes, 0% true NaN (verified). Heavily skewed to Emergency 53,990 (53.05%, pr 0.11523); next tier Elective 18,869 (18.54%, pr 0.10393) and Urgent 18,480 (18.16%, pr 0.1118). ~10.21% of rows are pseudo-missing administrative placeholders disguised as codes: NULL 5,291 (5.20%, pr 0.11075), Not Available 4,785 (4.70%, pr 0.10345), Not Mapped 320 (0.31%, pr 0.08438) — combined 10,396 rows. Genuinely-clinical rare tail Trauma Center 21 (0.02%, 0/21) and Newborn 10 (0.01%, 1/10) carries 1 positive total and is noise in an adult-diabetic cohort. All figures verified against admin_id_coded.json.
columns.missing_mechanism_rationale: True NaN is 0% because '?' was never used; missingness is hidden inside three integer codes (5/6/8, ~10.21% combined). This is R1-relevant: the value IS missing, disguised as a code, and must not be treated as a real admission type. Placeholder positive rates (NULL 0.11075 ~= base, Not Available 0.10345, Not Mapped 0.08438) sit at or just below the cohort base rate 0.1116, so missingness tracks observable admin/site recording habits rather than the outcome itself -> MAR, not MNAR. Caveat: with only an outcome-vs-rate check available we cannot fully exclude MNAR, but the near-base rates make a strong MNAR claim unsupported.
columns.target_relationship: Very weak. Per-level positive_rate is nearly flat around base 0.1116: Emergency 0.11523, Urgent 0.1118, Elective 0.10393, NULL 0.11075, Not Available 0.10345, Not Mapped 0.08438; target_lift only 1.033 (verified). Trauma Center (0/21) and Newborn (1/10) are zero/near-zero but n<=21, statistically unreliable. Little standalone discriminative power for 30-day readmission.
columns.recommended_strategy: Keep as low-cardinality categorical. Map integer codes to labels, then collapse the three pseudo-missing codes (NULL, Not Available, Not Mapped) into a single explicit 'Unknown' level (R1: do NOT leave them as real admission types, do NOT drop the rows). Collapse the rare clinical tail Trauma Center + Newborn into 'Other' (combined n=31). Resulting levels: Emergency, Elective, Urgent, Other, Unknown.
columns.imputation_rationale: No row-level imputation: there is no defensible 'true' admission type to fill, and the placeholders behave at base rate, so collapsing to an explicit Unknown bucket preserves the R1 fact-of-missingness without inventing a value. Rare clinical levels (n<=21) are statistically unstable and folded into Other to avoid overfit one-hot columns.
columns.modeling_transform: one-hot encode the 5 collapsed levels (Emergency, Elective, Urgent, Other, Unknown). Trees can take it as a small categorical; one-hot is safest given non-monotone, near-flat target rates. Do NOT use raw integer as numeric (false ordinal).
columns.leakage_or_caveat: No post-discharge leakage (R4 clean) — admission type is fixed at intake. Caveat: codes are non-contiguous integers with embedded missingness, so naive numeric ingestion would (a) treat NULL/Not Available/Not Mapped as a real category violating R1 and (b) impose a false ordinal scale on unordered codes.
alternatives_considered: Drop the column entirely given near-zero target_lift (1.033) — rejected: cheap to keep, may interact with other features in trees.
Keep NULL/Not Available/Not Mapped as distinct categories — rejected: violates R1 and they are statistically indistinguishable from base rate, adding noise.

columns.semantic_meaning: Integer-coded field for where/how the patient was discharged (home, SNF, home-health, rehab, psychiatric, ICF, LTCH, expired, hospice, AMA, transfer, 'still patient', etc.). Single most important administrative column: it is the R5 filter source (expired/hospice codes) AND the strongest outcome correlate (destination encodes frailty/care-intensity). Determined exactly at the discharge decision point, so legitimate time-wise (R4).
columns.value_interpretation: 26 distinct codes, 0% true NaN (verified). Skewed to 'Discharged to home' 60,234 (59.19%, pr 0.093), then SNF 13,954 (13.71%, pr 0.14662) and home-with-home-health 12,902 (12.68%, pr 0.12696). Pseudo-missing: NULL 3,691 (3.63%, pr 0.12436) + Not Mapped 989 (0.97%, pr 0.09302) = ~4.60%. R5 expired/hospice rows are visible here: Expired(11) 1,642, Hospice/home(13) 399, Hospice/medical-facility(14) 372, Expired-at-home-hospice(19) 8, Expired-in-facility-hospice(20) 2 = 2,423 rows, matching dataset discharge_filter (n_drop_rows=2423, pct_drop=2.381; n_expired=1652 via codes 11/19/20/21, n_hospice=771 via 13/14). Note the dataset taxonomy classifies codes 19/20 as kind='expired', not hospice. Long unstable tail: rehab 1,993 (pr 0.27697), inpatient-institution 1,184 (pr 0.20861), psychiatric 139 (pr 0.36691), Medicare swing bed 63 (pr 0.44444), admitted-as-inpatient 21 (pr 0.42857), 'Still patient'(25) 3 (pr 0.66667). All figures verified.
columns.missing_mechanism_rationale: 0% true NaN; ~4.60% pseudo-missing (NULL 3.63% + Not Mapped 0.97%). NULL pr 0.12436 is mildly ABOVE base (0.1116) and Not Mapped pr 0.09302 is below, so the two placeholder paths diverge — the fact of an unrecorded disposition is weakly informative but not strongly outcome-driven, consistent with site/coding-process MAR rather than MNAR. The expired/hospice 0.0-pr levels are NOT missingness; they are structurally-impossible-to-readmit outcomes removed by R5.
columns.target_relationship: Strongest of the three (target_lift 3.982, verified) and clinically coherent. 'Discharged to home' has the lowest rate among large levels (0.093, below base) while sicker destinations are elevated: home-health 0.12696, SNF 0.14662, ICF 0.12761, inpatient-institution 0.20861, rehab 0.27697, psychiatric 0.36691, Medicare swing bed 0.44444 (n=63), admitted-as-inpatient 0.42857 (n=21). The gradient is broadly increasing with care intensity but NOT strictly monotone: long-term-care hospital (LTCH) is 0.07282 (n=412), BELOW base, breaking a clean monotonic ordering. After R5 removes the 0.0-pr expired/hospice levels, the home-vs-institutional gradient is a powerful, legitimately-timed predictor.
columns.recommended_strategy: FIRST apply R5: drop rows with expired codes 11,19,20,21 and hospice codes 13,14 — 2,423 rows — before any modeling/imputation. ALSO consider excluding 'Still patient / expected to return'(25, n=3) from the modeling cohort: the patient has not actually been discharged, so a discharge-time decision point is ill-defined (its pr 0.667 on n=3 is noise anyway). Then map codes to labels and bucket by care intensity, treating LTCH on its own observed rate rather than forcing it into a high-intensity bucket: 'Home' (Discharged to home), 'Home+Care' (home health, home IV), 'SNF/ICF/Nursing' (SNF, ICF, Medicaid nursing, swing bed), 'LTCH' (long-term-care hospital — kept separate given its anomalously low 0.073 rate, or fold into Home+Care empirically by rate), 'Rehab/Psych/Inpatient-transfer' (rehab, psychiatric, inpatient-institution, short-term hospital, admitted-as-inpatient), 'AMA/Other' (Left AMA, outpatient transfers, federal facility, neonate), and 'Unknown' (NULL + Not Mapped). Keep Unknown explicit (R1). Let CV decide whether to split or merge the rate-anomalous LTCH bucket.
columns.imputation_rationale: No value imputation: an unknown disposition has no defensible fill and its rate (~0.124) sits near/slightly-above base, so an explicit Unknown bucket honors R1 (and that explicit Unknown IS a weak signal level, hence missing_is_signal=true). Care-intensity bucketing is driven by the observed positive_rate gradient, but bucketing must respect the empirical rate — NOT assume monotonicity — so the low-rate LTCH level is not blindly merged into elevated nursing/SNF buckets. Rare unstable codes (n as low as 3-21) are pooled into intensity-matched buckets so signal survives without overfitting tiny one-hot columns.
columns.modeling_transform: one-hot encode the care-intensity buckets, plus an explicit Unknown indicator. An ordinal scale is tempting given the broad rate trend but is NOT strictly defensible (LTCH violates monotonicity), so prefer one-hot; provide an ordinal variant only as a CV-tested alternative.
columns.leakage_or_caveat: Timing is fine under R4 — disposition is determined AT discharge. BUT flag a subtle borderline: SNF/rehab/psychiatric encode the care team's post-discharge PLAN, partly a proxy for the team's own follow-up intensity / clinician judgment rather than raw patient state — allowed (known at t=discharge) but document in the model card. R5-critical: failing to drop expired/hospice here trains on structurally-impossible negatives (pr exactly 0.0) and corrupts calibration. Additional flag the prior analyst missed: 'Still patient'(25) and outpatient-transfer codes (pr 0.0, n<=14) are edge cases where 'discharge' is ambiguous; document the cohort-definition decision.
alternatives_considered: Keep all 26 raw codes one-hot — rejected: 13+ levels have n<=63 and several have undefined/extreme rates (n=3 at 0.667), causing severe overfit; bucketing by care intensity is more robust.

Treat the integer code as a single ordinal numeric — rejected: codes are not ordered by severity (LTCH 0.073 sits below high-intensity neighbors), so raw numeric and even a hand-built ordinal impose a partly false scale.

Force LTCH into the SNF/ICF/Nursing high-intensity bucket — rejected/flagged: LTCH pr 0.07282 is below base and below SNF 0.147, so this would blunt the bucket's signal; keep separate or merge by empirical rate.

columns.semantic_meaning: Integer-coded field for the referral source / origin of the admission (Emergency Room, Physician Referral, Clinic/HMO Referral, Transfer from hospital/facility/SNF/critical-access, Court/Law Enforcement, birth-related sources), plus pseudo-missing codes NULL / Not Available / Not Mapped. Determined at intake, available at the discharge decision point; no post-discharge content (R4 clean).
columns.value_interpretation: 17 distinct codes, 0% true NaN (verified). Dominated by Emergency Room 57,494 (56.50%, pr 0.11688) and Physician Referral 29,565 (29.05%, pr 0.10587) — together ~85.5%. Pseudo-missing ~6.94%: NULL 6,781 (6.66%, pr 0.10411), Not Available 125 (0.12%, pr 0.104), Not Mapped 161 (0.16%, pr 0.13665). Transfer cluster: from hospital 3,187 (pr 0.09696), another facility 2,264 (pr 0.09364), SNF 855 (pr 0.11813), critical-access 8 (0.0), same-fac separate-claim 12 (pr 0.16667). Referral cluster: Clinic 1,104 (pr 0.10054), HMO 187 (pr 0.15508). Birth/forensic codes (Extramural Birth 2, Normal Delivery 2, Sick Baby 1, Court/Law 16 pr 0.125, Ambulatory Surgery Center 2) negligible in this adult-diabetic cohort. All figures verified.
columns.missing_mechanism_rationale: 0% true NaN; ~6.94% pseudo-missing (NULL 6.66% + Not Available 0.12% + Not Mapped 0.16% = verified 6.944%). Their positive rates (NULL 0.10411, Not Available 0.104, Not Mapped 0.13665) hover around base 0.1116, indicating the unrecorded source reflects intake-paperwork/site practices, not the outcome -> MAR not MNAR. Missing-itself is not predictive (the slightly elevated Not Mapped 0.137 sits on only n=161, unstable).
columns.target_relationship: Weak-to-modest (target_lift 1.39, verified). Large levels barely separate: Emergency Room 0.11688 (slightly above base), Physician Referral 0.10587, NULL 0.10411, transfer-from-hospital 0.09696, transfer-from-other-facility 0.09364. Lift comes from small levels — HMO Referral 0.15508 (n=187), same-fac transfer 0.16667 (n=12), Court/Law 0.125 (n=16) — too sparse to trust. Net: a mild ER-vs-referral signal at best, weaker/noisier than discharge_disposition_id but slightly more than admission_type_id.
columns.recommended_strategy: Map codes to labels, then collapse to a compact set: 'Emergency Room', 'Physician Referral', 'Clinic/HMO Referral' (Clinic + HMO), 'Transfer' (from hospital, another facility, SNF, critical-access, same-fac, ambulatory surgery), 'Other' (Court/Law, Extramural Birth, Normal Delivery, Sick Baby), and 'Unknown' (NULL + Not Available + Not Mapped). Keep Unknown explicit per R1; do not let NULL become a real source and do not drop the rows.
columns.imputation_rationale: No imputation of a 'true' source — unknown source has no defensible fill and behaves at base rate, so an explicit Unknown bucket satisfies R1. Birth/forensic and rare transfer codes (n as low as 1-16) are statistically meaningless individually and pooled into Transfer/Other to prevent unstable one-hot columns.
columns.modeling_transform: one-hot encode the ~6 collapsed levels. Given near-flat, non-monotone target rates, avoid ordinal encoding; let trees/LR use the indicators. Do NOT ingest raw integer as numeric (false ordinal).
columns.leakage_or_caveat: No R4 leakage — admission source is fixed at intake. Caveat: embedded pseudo-missing integer codes mean naive numeric ingestion would violate R1 and impose a false ordinal scale on unordered referral codes.
alternatives_considered: Reduce to a single binary 'admitted via ER vs not' — viable lightweight option since ER 0.11688 is the only large level above base, but loses the modest transfer/referral structure; keep the 6-level version and let CV/regularization prune.
Keep all 17 raw codes — rejected: 9 levels have n<=125 with unstable rates, adding noise.

columns.semantic_meaning: Administrative code for the primary payer / insurer responsible for the encounter (MC = Medicare, MD = Medicaid, BC = Blue Cross, HM = HMO, SP = Self-Pay, CP/CM/CH/OG/DM/etc = assorted commercial/government plans, UN = recorded-Unknown payer, FR = free/charity). It is a billing-system field, not a clinical one, captured at admission/registration. It is a proxy for insurance type and therefore for socioeconomic status, age cohort (Medicare skews elderly) and access-to-care.
columns.value_interpretation: 17 distinct observed levels among the 60.443% (61,510 rows) that are populated. Distribution is highly concentrated and long-tailed: MC (Medicare) 32,439 rows = 31.876% of all rows and 52.74% of populated rows, dwarfing every other level. Next tier: HM 6,274 (6.165%, positive_rate 0.10265), SP 5,007 (4.92%, 0.10186), BC 4,655 (4.574%, 0.09151), MD 3,532 (3.471%, 0.11778). Long tail: CP 2,533 (0.08448), UN 2,448 (0.09273), CM 1,937 (0.10222), OG 1,033 (0.13166), PO 592 (0.07432), DM 549 (0.11658), then rare CH 146 (0.08904), WC 135 (0.03704), OT 95 (0.07368), MP 79 (0.11392), SI 55 (0.12727), and a singleton FR count 1 positive_rate 0.0 (statistically meaningless). 'UN' (count 2,448) is an explicit recorded-Unknown payer code and must NOT be conflated with the '?'-derived missing value. Values are clean 2-letter codes; no numeric sentinels.
columns.missing_mechanism_rationale: 39.557% missing (40,256 rows loaded as NaN from '?'). Clearly NOT MCAR: 40% is far too high and the absence is structured, not random. MNAR is the leading hypothesis (institution/workflow-driven: the billing-registration process at certain facilities or for certain encounter types did not record a payer, or it was stripped during per-hospital de-identification), but I am explicit that MNAR is NOT PROVABLE from these data alone because the unobserved values cannot be inspected. The co-missingness signal the prior leaned on is genuinely weak: corr with A1Cresult -0.009 (essentially zero), with medical_specialty -0.137 and with max_glu_serum -0.119 (both weak negatives) — these magnitudes are too small to firmly establish a shared site-specific cause, so the mechanism is best described as MNAR-suspected with a partial MAR component (it tracks medical_specialty/site only weakly). Crucially, the OPERATIONAL handling (explicit Missing category + missingness indicator, no imputation) is identical under MNAR or MAR, so the modeling decision is robust to this uncertainty.
columns.target_relationship: Weak overall signal: target_lift 1.18, so the most predictive populated level barely separates the class. Per-level positive_rates hug the 11.16% base rate: MC 0.11745 (slightly elevated, dominant elderly-Medicare cohort), MD 0.11778, OG 0.13166 (highest non-rare), SI 0.12727 (n=55, noisy), vs lower commercial-ish codes CP 0.08448, BC 0.09151, UN 0.09273, PO 0.07432, WC 0.03704 (n=135, noisy), FR 0/1. Spread (~0.037 to ~0.132) is modest and mostly noise in the rare tail. The clinically meaningful contrast is Medicare/Medicaid (elderly/low-income, mildly higher) vs commercial. The missing-vs-present split likely carries as much signal as the level identity and should be retained via the indicator.
columns.recommended_strategy: Load with na_values='?' so '?' becomes NaN (R1). Do NOT drop the column and do NOT impute a payer. Add an explicit binary indicator payer_code_missing (1 if NaN). Collapse the 17 levels into a small financially-meaningful bucket set: Medicare = MC; Medicaid = MD; SelfPay = SP, PO; Unknown_recorded = UN (kept as its OWN level, NEVER merged with '?'); Commercial/Other = BC, HM, CP, CM, OG, DM, CH, WC, OT, SI, MP, and the FR singleton folded in here. The '?'-derived NaN becomes an explicit 'Missing' category. Note: MP is fold into Commercial/Other (NOT guessed into Medicaid as the prior tentatively wrote 'MP?') because its meaning is ambiguous and guessing fabricates a financial class. Result: ~6 stable buckets (Medicare/Medicaid/SelfPay/Unknown_recorded/Commercial-Other/Missing) plus the missing indicator.
columns.imputation_rationale: No imputation of an insurer is justifiable: a missing payer is not 'self-pay' nor 'Medicare', so fabricating one injects bias and is administratively false. The MNAR-suspected structure means the missingness pattern itself is informative, so the correct treatment is an explicit 'Missing' category plus a missingness indicator, preserving both the bucketed level information where present and the fact of absence. Collapsing the long tail avoids overfitting to rare 2-digit codes (FR n=1, SI n=55) under the patient-grouped split while retaining the Medicare/Medicaid/Commercial/SelfPay socioeconomic axis that has mild target signal.
columns.modeling_transform: One-hot encode the ~6 collapsed payer buckets (Medicare / Medicaid / SelfPay / Unknown_recorded / Commercial-Other / Missing) plus a separate payer_code_missing binary indicator. Given low lift (1.18), target-encoding is not worth the leakage-management overhead; one-hot of buckets is simpler, stable, and SHAP-readable. Carry an ablation that drops the bucketed levels but keeps the missing indicator, to check whether the level identity adds anything.
columns.leakage_or_caveat: No post-discharge leakage: payer is known at admission/registration and fully available at the discharge decision point (R4 satisfied). Fairness caveat: payer_code is a strong proxy for socioeconomic status and age/race; including it can encode disparate treatment, so it MUST be carried through the Fairlearn audit and flagged in the model card as a sensitive proxy. Do NOT let 'UN' or '?' silently collapse into one generic category (R1).
alternatives_considered: Drop the column entirely (rejected: the missingness indicator and the Medicare/Medicaid axis carry mild but real signal and the indicator is cheap)
Impute mode = MC (rejected: fabricates an insurer, biases toward Medicare, violates the spirit of R1)
Keep all 17 raw levels one-hot (rejected: rare codes FR n=1, SI n=55, WC n=135 overfit and are unstable across the patient-grouped split)
Target/mean encoding (rejected: low lift 1.18 does not justify the out-of-fold leakage-management overhead)
Fold MP into Medicaid as the prior tentatively suggested (rejected: MP meaning is ambiguous; guessing a financial class fabricates information)

columns.semantic_meaning: The medical specialty of the admitting/attending physician's department (e.g. InternalMedicine, Emergency/Trauma, Family/GeneralPractice, Cardiology, Nephrology, Oncology, Surgery-*). It is an administrative attribution recorded at admission and known by discharge, serving as a proxy for the clinical reason for admission and the care setting (Nephrology -> renal/dialysis comorbidity; Oncology -> cancer), so it correlates with disease severity and readmission risk.
columns.value_interpretation: Very high cardinality: 72 distinct populated levels (top 30 in facts) across the 50.918% (51,817 rows) that are populated. Strongly long-tailed and top-concentrated: InternalMedicine 14,635 (14.381%), Emergency/Trauma 7,565 (7.434%), Family/GeneralPractice 7,440 (7.311%), Cardiology 5,352 (5.259%), Surgery-General 3,099 (3.045%) account for the bulk; the remaining ~67 levels are each <2% and most <0.5% (e.g. Pediatrics-Endocrinology 159, Otolaryngology 125, Surgery-Cardiovascular 98, Pediatrics-CriticalCare 87) — a sparse tail prone to overfitting. No numeric sentinels; clean string labels. The 49.082% '?' must be kept distinct from any real specialty.
columns.missing_mechanism_rationale: 49.082% missing (49,949 rows from '?') — the largest categorical gap in this family. NOT MCAR (half the data, structured by pathway). MNAR is the leading hypothesis (process-driven: specialty was not attributed for certain admission routes such as some ED/hospitalist flows or specific facilities), but as with payer_code I flag that MNAR is NOT verifiable from the data because the unobserved attributions cannot be inspected. The co-missingness evidence is weak and mostly rules OUT a shared cause rather than proving site-specificity: corr with A1Cresult 0.018, max_glu_serum 0.018, weight 0.002 (all ~zero), payer_code -0.137 and diag_3 -0.045 (weak negatives) — i.e. it does not co-drop with another field. So the honest label is MNAR-suspected with a MAR component to the extent it tracks admission_type/site (which are observed). The operational handling (explicit Missing/Unknown category + indicator, no imputation) is identical under either, so the modeling choice is robust.
columns.target_relationship: Strongest signal in this family: target_lift 1.732, with per-level positive_rates fanning widely around the 11.16% base rate, separating high- vs low-risk care contexts. HIGH-risk: Hematology/Oncology 0.19324, Oncology 0.18966, Nephrology 0.15375, PhysicalMedicineandRehabilitation 0.15345, Surgery-Vascular 0.13884, Psychiatry 0.12178 (serious chronic/renal/cancer/rehab). LOW-risk: Pediatrics-Endocrinology 0.00629, Otolaryngology 0.016, Pediatrics-CriticalCare 0.03448, ObstetricsandGynecology 0.04769, Endocrinology 0.05, Neurology 0.05911, Pediatrics 0.06299, Surgery-Neuro 0.06838 (younger/elective/single-system). CORRECTION to the prior: the high-volume anchors do NOT all sit at base rate — InternalMedicine 0.11247, Emergency/Trauma 0.11183 and Family/GeneralPractice 0.11868 are at base rate, but Cardiology, the SECOND-largest anchor at 5,352 rows, has positive_rate 0.07941, materially BELOW the 11.16% base rate and is therefore a low/protective-risk anchor, not a base-rate one. This column is a meaningful predictor; both the high-risk specialties and the missing-vs-present split should be preserved.
columns.recommended_strategy: Load with na_values='?' (R1). Do NOT impute a specialty. Add a binary indicator medical_specialty_missing (1 if NaN), then collapse the 72 levels into a compact clinically-grouped bucket set rather than one-hot of all 72. Concrete scheme: (1) keep high-volume anchors as their own buckets: InternalMedicine, Emergency/Trauma, Family/GeneralPractice, Cardiology (note Cardiology is low-risk, 0.07941), Surgery-General; (2) group surgical subspecialties (Surgery-Vascular/-Neuro/-Thoracic/-Cardiovascular/-Cardiovascular-Thoracic, Orthopedics, Orthopedics-Reconstructive, Urology, Podiatry, etc.) into Surgical; (3) group high-risk medical subspecialties Nephrology + Oncology + Hematology/Oncology + Pulmonology + Gastroenterology into MedicalSubspecialty (optionally split a HighRisk Onco/Renal bucket given their strong lift); (4) group Pediatrics-*, ObGyn, Endocrinology, Neurology into a low-risk OtherClinical bucket; (5) collapse remaining rare (<~0.3%) levels into 'Other_Specialty'; (6) '?'-derived NaN becomes an explicit 'Missing/Unknown' category, kept SEPARATE from every real specialty. Target ~8-12 buckets + missing indicator.
columns.imputation_rationale: Imputing a specialty (e.g. most-common InternalMedicine) would invent attending attribution that did not exist, corrupt the strong Nephrology/Oncology high-risk signal, and falsely shrink the informative 'unknown care pathway' group. MNAR-suspected + missing-is-signal (the missing slice is half the data and is non-random by pathway) means 'Missing/Unknown' must be its own modeled category alongside an indicator. Bucketing the 72 levels is required because the patient-grouped split (16,773 multi-encounter patients, max 40 encounters) plus rare levels (many <100 rows) would otherwise overfit; grouping by clinical risk profile keeps the lift-1.732 signal while stabilizing variance.
columns.modeling_transform: One-hot encode the ~8-12 collapsed clinical buckets including an explicit 'Missing/Unknown' level, plus a separate medical_specialty_missing binary indicator. Given the high cardinality and meaningful lift, target/mean-encoding the collapsed buckets is a viable alternative ONLY with leakage-safe out-of-fold encoding computed inside the patient-grouped CV folds; default to one-hot of buckets for transparency and SHAP readability.
columns.leakage_or_caveat: No post-discharge leakage: the attending specialty is determined at/within the admission and is known at the discharge decision point (R4 satisfied). Caveat 1: do NOT one-hot all 72 raw levels — singletons/near-singletons plus the patient-grouped split make per-level estimates unstable. Caveat 2: keep '?'-derived Missing strictly separate from any real specialty (R1). Caveat 3: specialty correlates with the clinical reason for admission, so it partially encodes diagnosis information already present in diag_* — watch for redundancy (not leakage).
alternatives_considered: Drop entirely (rejected: lift 1.732 with clean high-risk Oncology/Nephrology separation is the best signal in this family)
Impute mode = InternalMedicine (rejected: fabricates attribution and destroys the high-risk-specialty signal)
One-hot all 72 raw levels (rejected: sparse tail + patient-grouped split overfits; many levels <100 rows)
Target-encode raw 72 levels directly (rejected unless done out-of-fold within grouped CV; default to bucketed one-hot for SHAP interpretability)
Treat Cardiology as a base-rate anchor like the prior implied (rejected: its 0.07941 rate is below base rate, so it is a low-risk anchor)

columns.semantic_meaning: Length of stay for the current encounter, in days (admission-to-discharge). Operational/severity proxy; longer stays generally mean sicker, more complex patients. Known by definition at the moment of discharge.
columns.value_interpretation: Integer, 14 distinct values 1-14 (capped at 14). Right-skewed (skew 1.134): min=p01=p05=1, p25=2, median=4, mean=4.396, p75=6, p95=11, p99=max=14, std=2.985. zeros_pct=0.0 (every stay >=1 day; no sentinel masquerading as a value). No suspicious codes. The 14 ceiling is a known right-censoring cap, not a natural maximum.
columns.missing_mechanism_rationale: n_missing=0; administratively derived from admit/discharge timestamps so always populated. No '?' so R1 is not engaged here.
columns.target_relationship: Monotone-ish positive gradient. positive_rate rises from 0.0914 in the (1,2]-day bin (below base 0.1116) to a peak 0.1403 in the (7,9] bin, then dips to 0.1276 in (9,14]. CORRECTED: target_lift 1.257 is (peak-bin rate 0.1403 / base rate 0.1116), NOT a max/min-bin ratio as the prior analyst stated; the naive max/min-bin ratio is ~1.53. Real but modest signal that plateaus/reverses at the 14-day cap.
columns.recommended_strategy: Keep as-is numeric; no imputation. Raw integer for XGBoost. For the LR baseline, model as ordinal or add a coarse bucket (1-2 / 3-4 / 5-7 / 8-14) to capture the non-monotone plateau at the cap. Add a FEATURE_LOG row noting the 14-day right-censoring so it is not mistaken for a natural ceiling.
columns.imputation_rationale: No missing values, so no imputation. The 14-day ceiling is a data-collection cap, not a quality issue; flag it, do not winsorize further.
columns.modeling_transform: Raw integer for trees; optional ordinal bucket (1-2/3-4/5-7/8-14) for the linear baseline.
columns.leakage_or_caveat: No leakage: length of stay is fully determined at discharge time (R4 satisfied). Caveats: right-censored at 14 days, so it cannot distinguish very long stays; the cited target_lift is a peak-bin/base-rate ratio, not max/min.
alternatives_considered: log1p transform (rejected - trees handle skew; range is tiny 1-14)
drop (rejected - real monotone-ish signal)

columns.semantic_meaning: Count of lab tests performed during the current encounter. Workup-intensity / diagnostic-effort proxy. Fully known at discharge.
columns.value_interpretation: Integer, 118 distinct values, 1-132. Roughly symmetric, slightly left-skewed (skew -0.237): p01=1, p05=4, p25=31, median=44, mean=43.10, p75=57, p95=73, p99=85, max=132, std=19.67. zeros_pct=0.0 (every encounter had >=1 lab, consistent with diabetic inpatients always getting metabolic panels). Long upper tail (max 132 vs p99 85) = a few heavily-worked-up cases; no obvious placeholder code.
columns.missing_mechanism_rationale: n_missing=0; a billing/order count, always recorded. No '?' values so R1 not engaged.
columns.target_relationship: Weak, mildly positive. positive_rate rises gently from 0.0981 in the (1,14] bin to 0.1228 in the (60,67] bin, then dips to 0.1158 in the top (67,132] bin (i.e. NOT monotone at the very top). CORRECTED: target_lift 1.10 = (peak-bin 0.1228 / base 0.1116), the weakest-but-one in the family. Mostly a severity/intensity correlate already captured by other utilization fields.
columns.recommended_strategy: Keep as numeric; no imputation; no transform for trees. Treat as a drop candidate ONLY if SHAP/collinearity with num_medications and time_in_hospital shows it is redundant and it hurts the linear baseline; otherwise retain. Document the 132 upper tail but do not cap.
columns.imputation_rationale: No missing values. Distribution is clean over a count; no sentinel handling required.
columns.modeling_transform: Raw integer; optionally standardize for the LR baseline (mean~43, std~20).
columns.leakage_or_caveat: No leakage - labs ordered during the stay and counted by discharge (R4 OK). Caveats: weakest individual signal and largely redundant with overall encounter intensity; cited lift is a peak-bin/base-rate ratio.
alternatives_considered: standardize (kept optional for LR only)
drop (deferred - small positive gradient, cheap to keep; re-evaluate via SHAP/collinearity)

columns.semantic_meaning: Count of (non-lab) procedures performed during the current encounter (e.g. surgical/interventional). Intensity/severity proxy. Known at discharge.
columns.value_interpretation: Integer, only 7 distinct values (0-6), effectively a small ordinal. Right-skewed (skew 1.316), zero-inflated: zeros_pct=45.84 (p01=p05=p25=0, median=1, mean=1.340, p75=2, p95=5, p99=6, max=6, std=1.706). Nearly half of encounters had no procedure - 0 is a genuine measured 'none', NOT a missing placeholder.
columns.missing_mechanism_rationale: n_missing=0; always recorded. R1 note: the 45.84% zeros are real 'no procedure performed', not disguised missing - must NOT be treated as NaN or recoded.
columns.target_relationship: Essentially flat / no usable gradient. positive_rate stays near base across all bins: 0.1143 (0-1), 0.1118 (1-2), 0.1069 (2-3), 0.1103 (3-4), 0.0952 (4-6) - if anything the highest-procedure bin readmits slightly LESS. CORRECTED: target_lift 1.024 = (peak-bin 0.1143 / base 0.1116); the single weakest predictor in the family. No monotone clinical story.
columns.recommended_strategy: Keep as numeric/ordinal for v1 completeness; no imputation; do NOT invent a missing-indicator (no missing). Strong candidate for dropping after the first model if SHAP confirms near-zero importance; flag in FEATURE_LOG that lift is ~1.0 and the gradient is flat/non-monotone.
columns.imputation_rationale: No missing values; zeros are legitimate counts and stay as 0.
columns.modeling_transform: Raw integer (ordinal 0-6); one-hot possible given only 7 levels but unnecessary.
columns.leakage_or_caveat: No leakage (procedures done during the stay, known by discharge - R4 OK). Caveat: essentially no discriminative power (lift 1.024, flat); expect ~0 SHAP importance.
alternatives_considered: drop immediately (deferred - keep for v1, drop if SHAP confirms uninformative)
treat 0 as missing (REJECTED - violates R1; 0 is a real 'no procedure')

columns.semantic_meaning: Count of distinct generic medications administered during the current encounter. Polypharmacy / treatment-complexity proxy tied to comorbidity burden. Known at discharge.
columns.value_interpretation: Integer, 75 distinct values, 1-81. Right-skewed (skew 1.327): p01=3, p05=6, p25=10, median=15, mean=16.02, p75=20, p95=31, p99=43, max=81, std=8.13. zeros_pct=0.0 (every encounter had >=1 med). Long upper tail (max 81 vs p99 43) = extreme-polypharmacy outliers; no placeholder code.
columns.missing_mechanism_rationale: n_missing=0; administrative med-administration count, always present. No '?' so R1 not engaged.
columns.target_relationship: Clear, fairly monotone positive gradient. positive_rate rises from 0.0812 (1-7 meds, well below base) through 0.1121 (13-15) to a peak 0.1316 (19-22), then plateaus ~0.125-0.128 in the highest bins. CORRECTED: target_lift 1.179 = (peak-bin 0.1316 / base 0.1116), NOT max/min (which is ~1.62). A solid moderate signal: more meds => higher readmit risk.
columns.recommended_strategy: Keep as numeric; no imputation. Raw integer for trees. Optional log1p or winsorize at ~p99 (43) for the LR baseline to tame the 81-max tail; trees need neither. Retain - one of the better moderate predictors here.
columns.imputation_rationale: No missing values. The right tail is real polypharmacy, not error; cap only for linear models, do not delete rows.
columns.modeling_transform: Raw integer for trees; optional log1p / winsorize at p99 and standardize for the linear baseline.
columns.leakage_or_caveat: No leakage - meds given during the stay, counted by discharge (R4 OK). Expected mild collinearity with time_in_hospital and num_lab_procedures (all index overall encounter intensity); cited lift is peak-bin/base-rate.
alternatives_considered: log1p (optional, for skew)
winsorize p99 (optional, for LR tail)
drop (rejected - clear monotone signal)

columns.semantic_meaning: Number of outpatient visits by the patient in the period preceding the current encounter (documented as the prior year in the UCI data dictionary). A prior-healthcare-utilization / chronic-management signal, measured before admission.
columns.value_interpretation: Integer, 39 distinct values, 0-42. Extremely right-skewed (skew 8.83), heavily zero-inflated: zeros_pct=83.55 (min=p01=p05=p25=median=p75=0, mean=0.369, p95=2, p99=5, max=42, std=1.27). The vast majority had zero prior outpatient visits; 0 is a genuine measured value, NOT missing.
columns.missing_mechanism_rationale: n_missing=0; derived from prior claims, always computed. R1 note: the 83.55% zeros are real 'no prior outpatient visits', not disguised NaN - do not recode to missing.
columns.target_relationship: Weak but real positive signal. Only 2 facts bins, and CORRECTED: the low bin (-0.001,1.0] contains BOTH value 0 AND value 1 (count 93,574, positive_rate 0.1097, ~base); the high bin (1,42] (i.e. >=2 visits) has positive_rate 0.1332 (n=8,192). target_lift 1.193 = (high-bin 0.1332 / base 0.1116). So the small lift comes from patients with >=2 prior outpatient visits, NOT from (>0).
columns.recommended_strategy: Keep numeric. CORRECTED helper: because the binned signal separates at >=2 (the low bin folds 0 and 1 together), prefer prior_outpatient_2plus = (number_outpatient >= 2) OR a bucket {0 / 1 / 2+}, rather than a simple has_prior_outpatient=(>0) flag that does not match where the lift lives. No imputation; bucketing addresses the 8.8 skew. Document in FEATURE_LOG.
columns.imputation_rationale: No missing values; zeros are legitimate. Bucketing/capping addresses extreme skew, not missingness.
columns.modeling_transform: Bucket {0 / 1 / 2+} (or capped count at p99=5) for LR; raw integer for trees.
columns.leakage_or_caveat: Documented as a PRE-admission prior-period count, so knowable at discharge (R4 OK) IF the prior-year semantics hold. CAVEAT: these facts contain no timestamp proving the window is strictly pre-admission - confirm against the data dictionary before trusting R4. Severe zero-inflation makes the mean meaningless; treat as count.
alternatives_considered: prior_outpatient_2plus (>=2) flag (recommended - matches the binned signal)
has_prior_outpatient (>0) (rejected - lift lives at >=2, not >0)
log1p
combine into prior_visits_total with emergency+inpatient

columns.semantic_meaning: Number of emergency-department visits by the patient in the period preceding the current encounter (documented as the prior year). A prior-instability / acute-utilization signal, measured before admission.
columns.value_interpretation: Integer, 33 distinct values, 0-76. Pathologically right-skewed (skew 22.86), extremely zero-inflated: zeros_pct=88.81 (min through p75 all 0, mean=0.198, p95=1, p99=3, max=76, std=0.93). Almost 9 in 10 had no prior ER visit; 0 is real 'no ER visits', not missing. max=76 vs p99=3 is an extreme tail (a handful of frequent-ER patients).
columns.missing_mechanism_rationale: n_missing=0; derived from prior claims. R1 note: the 88.81% zeros are legitimate 'no prior ER visits', not disguised NaN.
columns.target_relationship: Strong positive signal despite rarity. CORRECTED bin membership: the low bin (-0.001,1.0] folds value 0 AND value 1 together (n=98,060, positive_rate 0.1078, ~base); the high bin (1,76] is patients with >=2 ER visits (n=3,706) at positive_rate 0.2126 - nearly double base. target_lift 1.905 = (high-bin 0.2126 / base 0.1116). KEY: the doubling is for >=2 prior ER visits, NOT >=1.
columns.recommended_strategy: Keep numeric. CORRECTED helper: use prior_emergency_2plus = (number_emergency >= 2) OR bucket {0 / 1 / 2+} so the flag matches where the lift actually lives; a has_prior_emergency=(>0) flag would dilute the signal because value-1 patients sit at ~base rate in the low bin. No imputation; cap/bucket handles skew=22.9. Prioritize in FEATURE_LOG.
columns.imputation_rationale: No missing values; zeros legitimate. Capping/bucketing handles the skew tail, not missingness.
columns.modeling_transform: Bucket {0 / 1 / 2+} (or cap at p99=3) for LR; raw integer for trees.
columns.leakage_or_caveat: Documented as a PRE-admission prior-period count (R4 OK) IF prior-year semantics hold - confirm against the data dictionary, as these facts carry no timestamp. CAVEAT: signal lives in <4% of rows, so aggregate importance can look small even though per-patient lift is high; the >=2 flag makes it robust.
alternatives_considered: prior_emergency_2plus (>=2) flag (recommended - matches binned signal)
has_prior_emergency (>0) (rejected - value-1 patients are at base rate)
cap at p99=3
combine into prior_visits_total

columns.semantic_meaning: Number of inpatient (hospital admission) stays by the patient in the period preceding the current encounter (documented as the prior year). The strongest single readmission signal in this family - prior admissions are the textbook predictor of future admissions. Measured before this encounter.
columns.value_interpretation: Integer, 21 distinct values, 0-21. Strongly right-skewed (skew 3.61), zero-inflated: zeros_pct=66.46 (min through median=0, mean=0.636, p75=1, p95=3, p99=6, max=21, std=1.26). Two-thirds had no prior inpatient stay; 0 is real 'no prior admissions', not missing. Tail (max 21 vs p99 6) flags chronic high-utilizers.
columns.missing_mechanism_rationale: n_missing=0; prior-period admission count, always computed. R1 note: the 66.46% zeros are legitimate 'no prior inpatient stays', not disguised NaN.
columns.target_relationship: Strongest predictor in the family, clean monotone gradient. CORRECTED bin membership: bin (-0.001,1.0] = values {0,1} (n=87,151, rate 0.0944, below base); bin (1,2] = value {2} (n=7,566, rate 0.1743); bin (2,21] = values {3..21} (n=7,049, rate 0.2566). target_lift 2.30 = (top-bin 0.2566 / base 0.1116) - the highest of all 8 columns. (The prior analyst also quoted ~2.7x, which is the top-bin/low-bin ratio 0.2566/0.0944=2.72 - a different and looser statistic; the authoritative facts lift is 2.30 vs base.)
columns.recommended_strategy: Keep raw integer for trees. CORRECTED helper: because value 1 is folded into the lowest (below-base) bin, a has_prior_inpatient=(>0) flag straddles the threshold and weakens it; prefer an ordinal bucket {0 / 1 / 2 / 3+} (or >=2 / >=3 flags) for the linear baseline so the monotone gradient is exposed at the right cut points. No imputation. Marquee feature - highlight in FEATURE_LOG and the model card; anchor of an optional prior_visits_total composite.
columns.imputation_rationale: No missing values; zeros legitimate. Bucketing exposes the strong monotone gradient to LR; trees capture it natively.
columns.modeling_transform: Raw integer for trees; ordinal bucket {0/1/2/3+} for LR (preferred over a single >0 flag).
columns.leakage_or_caveat: Documented as a PRE-admission prior-period count (R4 OK) IF prior-year semantics hold - confirm against the data dictionary, as these facts carry no timestamp proving the window is strictly pre-admission. High collinearity with number_emergency (both index frequent-utilizer patients) - keep both, watch LR coefficients. FAIRNESS WATCH: prior-utilization features can encode access-to-care disparities by race/payer - audit in the fairness step.
alternatives_considered: ordinal bucket {0/1/2/3+} (recommended for LR - matches bin cut points)
has_prior_inpatient (>0) (de-emphasized - value 1 sits below base)
prior_visits_total composite
drop (REJECTED - strongest predictor)

columns.semantic_meaning: Number of diagnoses entered for the current encounter (count of coded diagnoses on the record). Comorbidity-burden / case-complexity proxy. Known at discharge.
columns.value_interpretation: Integer, 16 distinct values, 1-16. Left-skewed (skew -0.877) with a hard administrative ceiling at 9: p01=2, p05=4, p25=6, median=8, mean=7.42, p75=p95=p99=9, max=16, std=1.93. zeros_pct=0.0. Striking pile-up at 9: the (8,9] bin holds 49,474 rows (48.6% of data) because the source coding system caps recorded diagnoses at 9, so '9' effectively means '9 or more'. Only 115 rows (0.1%) exceed 9.
columns.missing_mechanism_rationale: n_missing=0; always populated. The clustering at 9 is a coding cap, not missingness; do not treat it as a sentinel. R1 not engaged.
columns.target_relationship: Positive gradient up to the cap. positive_rate rises from 0.0849 (1-5 diagnoses, below base) through 0.1077 (6-7) and 0.1181 (7-8) to 0.1238 in the giant (8,9] bin. CORRECTED: target_lift 1.325 = (top-bin 0.14783 / base 0.1116), but that top bin is the (9,16] group of ONLY 115 rows whose 95% CI is 0.083-0.213 and CONTAINS the base rate - i.e. that headline lift is statistically fragile and NOT a reliable 'solid moderate signal'. The ROBUST signal is the 0.1238 in the 49k-row (8,9] bin -> lift ~1.11 vs base. So the column is a genuine but only WEAK-to-moderate predictor, weaker than the prior analyst's 1.325 framing implies.
columns.recommended_strategy: Keep numeric; no imputation. Treat the 9-cap explicitly ('9' = '9+'): a coarse ordinal bucket {1-4 / 5-6 / 7-8 / 9+} for the LR baseline, documenting the censoring in FEATURE_LOG. Trees handle the cap natively. Do NOT special-case or rely on the 115 rows >9 (statistically noisy). Folding them into a '9+' level is appropriate.
columns.imputation_rationale: No missing values; the value 9 is a legitimate (censored) count, not a placeholder, so it stays as-is.
columns.modeling_transform: Raw integer for trees; ordinal bucket {1-4/5-6/7-8/9+} for LR to reflect the censoring at 9.
columns.leakage_or_caveat: No leakage - diagnosis count is finalized by discharge (R4 OK). CAVEATS: ~49% of rows pile at the value 9 due to the coding cap (interpret '9' as '9-or-more'); the cited target_lift 1.325 is driven by a 115-row bin whose CI overlaps the base rate, so the robust effect size is ~1.11, not 1.325.
alternatives_considered: ordinal bucket {1-4/5-6/7-8/9+} (recommended for LR, encodes the cap and absorbs the noisy >9 tail)
treat 9 as a separate 'capped' category (overkill for trees)
drop (rejected - weak but real monotone signal up to the cap)

columns.semantic_meaning: Primary/principal diagnosis for the encounter, ICD-9-CM, recorded for the stay and known at discharge. The single most clinically informative of the three diagnosis slots about WHY the patient was hospitalized; strongest separator of the three.
columns.value_interpretation: Confirmed against the facts file: 716 distinct codes over 101,766 rows, distribution concentrated in cardio-pulmonary and diabetes codes. Top levels: 428 (CHF) 6,862/6.743% (readmit 14.11%), 414 (chronic ischemic heart disease) 6,581/6.467% (readmit 9.04%, BELOW base rate), 786 (chest/respiratory symptoms) 4,016/3.946% (7.25%), 410 (acute MI) 3,614/3.551% (10.32%), 486 (pneumonia) 3,508/3.447% (8.95%). Codes are stored as STRINGS with embedded decimals (250.8, 250.6, 250.7, 250.13) and V/E prefixes (V57 rehab 1,207/1.186%), so naive numeric casting would corrupt them. 716-level long tail with hundreds of codes appearing only a few times. NOTE: the facts file does NOT report a positive_rate for the missing level, so no missing-vs-present risk comparison can be made from ground truth.
columns.missing_mechanism_rationale: Only 21 of 101,766 rows (0.021%) lack a primary diagnosis; a hospitalized patient essentially always has a principal billing diagnosis, so absence reflects sporadic record/coding gaps. diag_1 is NOT in the dataset co-missing block (only diag_3 of the three is), so its missingness does not co-vary with race/weight/payer/specialty/labs. At n=21 the mechanism is empirically indistinguishable; MCAR is the only defensible (and lowest-assumption) label and the practical impact is negligible.
columns.target_relationship: Strongest separator of the three diag fields: target_lift 1.698 (verified). Diabetes-with-complication primaries carry the highest 30-day readmit rates: 250.7 (diabetes w/ peripheral circulatory) 18.94%, 250.6 (diabetes w/ neuro) 18.51%, 434 (cerebral artery occlusion/stroke) 16.22%, 820 (hip fracture) 15.80% — all well above the 11.16% base rate. Low-risk primaries: 786 (chest symptoms) 7.25%, 435 (TIA) 7.68%, 574 (gallstones) 8.71%. High-volume CHF (428) sits at 14.11%. A top predictor once bucketed. (Note: not every commonly-listed circulatory primary is high-risk — 414 is 9.04%, below base rate — so chapter buckets, not 'cardio = high risk', are the right abstraction.)
columns.recommended_strategy: Keep the column and BUCKET it into the ICD-9 diagnosis-chapter scheme used in Strack et al. 2014 on this exact dataset (~9 chapters + Diabetes + Other). Parse the string code: route V/E codes to a dedicated 'V/E' bucket; for numeric 3-digit prefixes assign Circulatory (390-459), Respiratory (460-519), Digestive (520-579), Diabetes (any 250.xx), Injury (800-999), Musculoskeletal (710-739), Genitourinary (580-629), Neoplasms (140-239), Other (everything else). For the 21 missing rows add an explicit 'diag_missing' level so '?' never survives as a literal token and the missingness fact is preserved (R1); with only 21 rows it may safely fold into Other if a separate level is undesirable. Fit ONE shared code-to-bucket map in the cleaning pipeline so train/val/test use identical rules and test-only codes route deterministically to Other.
columns.imputation_rationale: No value-imputation: a diagnosis cannot be meaningfully filled in, and 21 rows are immaterial. An explicit missing flag preserves R1 at zero cost. Bucketing instead of one-hot over 716 raw codes prevents catastrophic sparsity, high-cardinality overfitting, and train/test level mismatch.
columns.modeling_transform: One-hot the ~10-11 ICD-9 chapter buckets (Circulatory, Respiratory, Digestive, Diabetes, Injury, Musculoskeletal, Genitourinary, Neoplasms, V/E, Other [+ diag_missing if kept]). Optionally add a binary 'primary_is_diabetes' flag. For tree models, target/ordinal encoding of the bucket is viable but MUST be fit on the TRAINING fold only of the patient-grouped (GroupShuffleSplit) split and applied to val/test, to avoid label leakage.
columns.leakage_or_caveat: No post-discharge leakage (R4): the principal diagnosis reflects the condition responsible for the stay and is known at the discharge decision point. Caveat: ICD-9 billing codes may be finalized shortly after the encounter, but the diagnostic FACT exists at decision time, so it is admissible. The real risk is encoding leakage: any target/frequency encoder must be fit within-fold on the patient-grouped split, never on the full dataset.
alternatives_considered: One-hot all 716 raw codes (rejected: extreme sparsity, train/test level mismatch, overfitting)
Group only top-N codes and lump the rest (rejected vs. clinically-meaningful ICD-9 chapter bucketing, which is more interpretable and stable)
Drop the 21 missing rows (rejected: violates R1 spirit to preserve the missingness fact; explicit category is cheaper)
Target-encode raw codes directly (kept as a tree-model option, within-fold only)

columns.semantic_meaning: Secondary diagnosis (ICD-9-CM): an additional/comorbid condition coded for the encounter beyond the principal diagnosis. Captures comorbidity burden (hypertension, electrolyte disorders, diabetes as a non-principal condition, renal disease) that modulates readmission risk.
columns.value_interpretation: Verified: 748 distinct codes, slightly higher cardinality than diag_1. Dominated by chronic-comorbidity codes rather than acute presentations: 276 (fluid/electrolyte) 6,752/6.635%, 428 (CHF) 6,662/6.546%, 250 (diabetes, unspecified) 6,071/5.966%, 427 (dysrhythmias) 5,036/4.949%, 401 (essential hypertension) 3,736/3.671%. Decimal diabetes subtypes (250.02, 250.01, 250.6) and renal codes (585 CKD, 403 hypertensive CKD, 584 ARF) are prominent. Same string-with-decimals/V-code format; 748-level long tail. IMPORTANT: the facts file provides NO positive_rate for the missing/empty level, so the prior claim that an empty secondary slot is itself a risk-distinguishing signal is NOT verifiable from ground truth.
columns.missing_mechanism_rationale: CORRECTED from MAR to MCAR. Only 358 of 101,766 rows (0.352%) are missing. diag_2 is NOT in the dataset co-missing block, so there is no measured co-variation of its missingness with race/weight/payer/specialty/labs or with diag_3. MAR requires the missingness to depend on OBSERVED variables; the prior 'missing = simpler/shorter stay = lower complexity' story is an unverified causal narrative with no supporting statistic in the facts file (no complexity proxy is shown to drive it). At 0.35% missing with no evidence of dependence on observed fields, MCAR is the lowest-assumption defensible label. Handling is unchanged either way, so this correction does not alter the recommendation.
columns.target_relationship: Weakest separator of the three but still meaningful: target_lift 1.452 (verified). Per-level readmit rates in the top 30 span ~7% to ~16%: highest are 250.6 (diabetes w/ neuro) 16.20%, 403 (hypertensive CKD) 15.20%, 585 (CKD) 14.54%, 707 (chronic skin ulcer) 14.26% — renal and diabetic-complication comorbidities drive risk up. Lowest: 401 (hypertension) 7.17%, 250 (diabetes unspecified) 7.35%, 786 (chest symptoms) 7.45%, 493 (asthma) 7.95%. A renal/diabetic comorbidity in the secondary slot is a useful risk amplifier. Whether the EMPTY secondary slot is itself informative is unverified by the facts file (no missing-level positive_rate), so it should be treated as a hypothesis, not an established signal.
columns.recommended_strategy: Bucket identically to diag_1 using ONE shared, pipeline-fitted ICD-9 chapter map across all three diag fields (Circulatory / Respiratory / Digestive / Diabetes / Injury / Musculoskeletal / Genitourinary / Neoplasms / V-E / Other). For the 358 missing rows, create an explicit 'diag2_none/missing' level so '?' never becomes a literal token and the missingness fact is preserved (R1). Keep it as its own level (cheap, R1-compliant); the empty slot may carry the clinically plausible 'no coded secondary comorbidity = lower burden' meaning, but treat that as a hypothesis to validate within-fold rather than an asserted signal.
columns.imputation_rationale: Do not impute a clinical code into an empty comorbidity slot — that would fabricate a comorbidity the patient does not have (and violate R1 intent). An explicit 'no secondary diagnosis' level respects R1 and lets the model learn any genuine difference if one exists, without the analyst pre-committing to an unverified 'lower complexity' interpretation.
columns.modeling_transform: One-hot the ~10-11 buckets including an explicit 'diag2_none/missing' level. Optionally derive cross-field comorbidity features over diag_1..diag_3 jointly — total comorbidity count, 'has_renal_comorbidity', 'has_diabetes_complication' booleans — which often outperform the raw bucket for tree models. Any target encoding must be fit within the training fold of the patient-grouped split.
columns.leakage_or_caveat: No post-discharge leakage (R4): secondary diagnoses are part of the same discharge coding and reflect conditions present during the stay. Same encoding-leakage caution as diag_1: fit any frequency/target encoder within-fold on GroupShuffleSplit folds. Codes in the secondary slot reflect coder/hospital conventions (noise, not leakage).
alternatives_considered: Treat missing as the Other bucket (rejected: an explicit none/missing level is cheaper and R1-cleaner, and lets the model test for a 'no comorbidity' effect)
One-hot all 748 raw codes (rejected: sparsity / level-mismatch / overfitting)
Drop diag_2 since lift is lowest (rejected: 1.452 lift is still useful and it powers comorbidity-count features)
Mean/mode imputation of the code (rejected: fabricates a comorbidity, violates clinical meaning and R1 intent)
Assign MAR with a 'lower-complexity' story (rejected: no supporting statistic; not in co-missing block; MCAR is the lower-assumption call)

columns.semantic_meaning: Third diagnosis slot (ICD-9-CM), capturing further comorbidity load. Of the three it is the most often empty because not every encounter codes a third condition (highest missingness: 1.398%).
columns.value_interpretation: Verified: highest cardinality of the three, 789 distinct codes. Dominated by chronic comorbidities even more than diag_2: 250 (diabetes unspecified) is the single largest level at 11,555/11.354% (readmit 8.52%), then 401 (hypertension) 8,289/8.145% (8.30%), 276 (fluid/electrolyte) 5,175/5.085%, 428 (CHF) 4,577/4.498%, 427 (dysrhythmias) 3,955/3.886%, 414 (chronic IHD) 3,664/3.600%. Renal codes (403 hypertensive CKD 2,357, 585 CKD 1,992, 584 ARF 963) and lipid disorder 272 (1,969/1.935%) feature heavily; V45 status-post codes 1,389/1.365%. Heavy concentration of 250 and 401 reflects chronic diabetes/hypertension routinely listed as tertiary comorbidities. Same string format; 789-level long tail. The facts file gives NO positive_rate for the empty/missing level.
columns.missing_mechanism_rationale: CORRECTED from MAR to MCAR, with a fixed reading of the co-missingness matrix. diag_3 (1,423/101,766 = 1.398% missing) IS the only diag field in the co-missing block (row index 4). Its correlations with the other co-missing fields, read directly, are: race +0.024, weight +0.010, payer_code +0.038, medical_specialty -0.045 (the prior analysis reported this as +0.045 'with medical_specialty' as the strongest POSITIVE — the sign was wrong; it is the strongest NEGATIVE and still trivially small), max_glu_serum -0.003, A1Cresult -0.037. ALL are near zero, so diag_3 missingness does NOT co-cluster with the heavily-missing lab/payer/weight/specialty fields. That near-independence rules out MNAR clustering, but it also REMOVES the only evidence one could use to argue MAR (dependence on an observed field): there is no observed correlate driving the missingness. The prior 'missing = lower complexity / shorter stay → MAR' is an unsupported causal story. With no demonstrated dependence on observed variables and no MNAR clustering, MCAR is the lowest-assumption defensible label. Handling is unchanged either way.
columns.target_relationship: Moderate separator: target_lift 1.593 (verified, between diag_1 and diag_2). Top-30 per-level readmit rates span ~6.9% to ~17.8%. Highest are renal/diabetic: 250.6 (diabetes w/ neuro) 17.78%, 585 (CKD) 16.87%, 403 (hypertensive CKD) 16.33%, 707 (chronic ulcer) 14.85%, 496 (chronic airway obstruction) 13.44%. Lowest: 272 (lipid disorder) 6.86%, 278 (overweight/obesity) 7.50%, 41 (bacterial infection) 7.98%, 401 (hypertension) 8.30%, 250 (diabetes unspecified) 8.52%. A renal/diabetic-complication tertiary comorbidity raises risk; a lipid/HTN-only profile lowers it. Whether the ~1.4% EMPTY slot is itself low-risk is unverified by the facts file (no missing-level positive_rate), so 'missing marks the lowest-comorbidity encounters' is a hypothesis, not an established result.
columns.recommended_strategy: Bucket with the SAME shared ICD-9 chapter map applied to diag_1 and diag_2 (Circulatory / Respiratory / Digestive / Diabetes / Injury / Musculoskeletal / Genitourinary / Neoplasms / V-E / Other) so the three fields encode consistently. For the 1,423 missing rows, assign an explicit 'diag3_none/missing' level so '?' never persists as a literal code and the missingness fact is preserved (R1). KEEP it as its own level: at 1.4% it is worth an explicit level (and a missing-indicator if engineering cross-field comorbidity-count features), and it lets the model test for any 'no third comorbidity' effect within-fold. Pipeline-fit the map so val/test reuse it and unseen codes route to Other.
columns.imputation_rationale: An empty third-diagnosis slot means 'no third comorbidity coded', not an unknown value to guess; imputing any code fabricates disease and violates R1 intent. An explicit none/missing level satisfies R1 at near-zero cost. The 1.4% rate is large enough that a missing-indicator (and a comorbidity-count derived feature) is worthwhile rather than folding silently into Other.
columns.modeling_transform: One-hot the ~10-11 buckets plus an explicit 'diag3_none/missing' level. Best practice: combine diag_1/2/3 buckets into engineered cross-field features — total comorbidity count (3 minus number of empty slots), 'any_renal_diagnosis', 'any_diabetes_complication' booleans — which compress the three high-card fields into stable, interpretable predictors. Fit any target encoding within the training fold of the GroupShuffleSplit only.
columns.leakage_or_caveat: No post-discharge leakage (R4): tertiary diagnoses are part of the encounter's discharge coding. The co-missingness note matters for the broader pipeline — diag_3 missingness is essentially INDEPENDENT (all |corr| <= 0.045) of the lab/payer/weight/specialty missingness, so do NOT co-impute them with a shared model; treat them as separate mechanisms. For diag_3 itself the only handling caution is within-fold encoder fitting on patient-grouped splits to avoid label leakage.
alternatives_considered: Fold missing into Other bucket (rejected: at 1.4% an explicit none/missing level + missing-indicator is cheap and R1-cleaner)
One-hot all 789 raw codes (rejected: worst sparsity of the three)
Co-impute diag_3 missingness with the lab/payer co-missing fields (rejected: all co-missingness correlations <= |0.045|, no shared mechanism to exploit)
Drop diag_3 as redundant with diag_1/2 (rejected: 1.593 lift and distinct renal-comorbidity signal; strengthens comorbidity-count features)
Assign MAR with a 'lower-complexity' story (rejected: no observed correlate drives the missingness; MCAR is the lower-assumption call)

columns.semantic_meaning: Result of a glucose serum (blood sugar) lab test ordered during the encounter, discretized into three clinical bands: Norm (in-range), >200 mg/dL (elevated), and >300 mg/dL (markedly elevated). A NaN means the test was simply not ordered during the stay, not that glucose was normal. It is a point-in-time inpatient observation available at the discharge decision point.
columns.value_interpretation: Extremely sparse: 96,420 of 101,766 rows (94.747%) are missing (test not ordered), verified against lab_result.json. Of the 5.253% measured, the distribution is Norm 2,597 (2.552%), >200 1,485 (1.459%), >300 1,264 (1.242%) — all exact per file. n_unique=3, is_constant=false, but the column is effectively dominated by the not-measured state. Among the measured bands, positive_rate rises monotonically with glucose severity: Norm 0.11359 -> >200 0.12458 -> >300 0.14320 (all exact per file). The file's target_lift=1.283 is the ratio of the most-extreme band to the OVERALL base rate (0.14320/0.1116 = 1.283), NOT the >300/Norm within-measured ratio (which is 1.261); the prior analyst mis-stated this denominator. 'Norm' is a genuine in-range measurement, not a missing surrogate, because the NaN already carries the not-measured meaning.
columns.missing_mechanism_rationale: Ordering a glucose serum test is a clinician decision driven by suspected hyperglycemia/clinical concern, so whether the value is observed depends on the (unobserved) underlying glucose state and acuity — the textbook MNAR pattern, codified by R3 (NaN = 'not ordered', not 'Norm'). The co-missingness block (verified in _dataset.json) supports a column-specific clinical mechanism rather than a shared data-export artifact: max_glu_serum-missing correlates only weakly with the heavy administrative-missing columns (payer_code -0.119, medical_specialty 0.018) and barely with the other lab A1Cresult (-0.07). The mild negative coupling with payer_code-missing hints at a small MAR/administrative component, but the dominant driver is the unobserved clinical reason to test, so MNAR is the correct primary label. 94.7% missing rules out MCAR.
columns.target_relationship: Two distinct, file-verified signals. (1) Measured-vs-not: measured rows have positive_rate 0.12364 (661/5,346) vs not-measured 0.11093 (10,696/96,420) — a derived +1.3pp lift, so 'a glucose test was ordered' weakly flags higher 30-day readmission. (2) Among measured rows, a clean monotonic dose-response: 0.11359 (Norm) -> 0.12458 (>200) -> 0.14320 (>300). The within-measured >300/Norm ratio is 1.261 (the file's headline target_lift 1.283 is >300/base_rate). Real and directionally clinical but thin — only 5.253% of rows are measured and the absolute separation is a few percentage points, so on its own a weak-to-moderate predictor; main value is an ordinal severity flag plus the binary 'tested' indicator.
columns.recommended_strategy: Load with na_values='?' so the raw '?' becomes NaN (R1). Do NOT impute and do NOT collapse NaN into 'Norm' (R3). Fill NaN with an explicit literal level 'NotMeasured' (or 'Not_Ordered') so the not-measured state is its own first-class category, yielding {NotMeasured, Norm, >200, >300}. Also emit a redundant binary indicator max_glu_serum_measured (1 if any of Norm/>200/>300, else 0) so a linear baseline can pick up the verified tested-vs-not signal directly. Never let '?' survive as a category by accident.
columns.imputation_rationale: No statistical imputation is justified: missingness is MNAR and informative (R3), so mean/mode/Norm imputation would destroy the strongest piece of information (whether the test was ordered) and falsely assert normoglycemia for 94.7% of encounters. The only defensible 'fill' is to make missingness explicit as its own category, preserving both the not-ordered signal and the ordinal severity of the measured bands.
columns.modeling_transform: Treat as an ordered categorical with natural clinical order NotMeasured (off-scale level) < Norm < >200 < >300. For tree models (XGBoost/LightGBM) one-hot encode the four levels; for the LR baseline one-hot encode and add the max_glu_serum_measured indicator. If a compact ordinal is desired, map Norm=0, >200=1, >300=2 with a separate measured flag rather than forcing NotMeasured onto the same numeric axis.
columns.leakage_or_caveat: No R4 leakage: the glucose value is observed during the stay and known at discharge.
